text:
The Olympic Gamesrepresent the pinnacle of athletic achievement. There is so much pressure, high expectations and unbelievable comebacks which often leave us reduced to tears.

A good part of the games which makes it so addictive are the stories. Over the years, we've delved into the lives of athletes from around the world and their personal narratives.

Stories of coming back from injury, returning from retirement, tales of struggle and strength - the list goes on. The Olympics have produced a plethora of emotional moments which have kept us glued to the television screen.

Remember - these are the Olympic Games and leaving empty-handed is part and parcel of the world of professional sports. Not everyone will be victorious.

Here are ten emotional stories that have come from the history of the Olympic Games.



Gemma Gibbons's London 2012 message

British judoka Gemma Gibbons shot to stardom not for her silver medal in London 2012, but for famously making the entire nation cry during her semi-final.

After making it to the final, she broke into tears as she looked to the heavens, mouthing the words: "I love you Mum." She was sending a message to her mother who passed away in 2004 after suffering from leukaemia.

Muhammad Ali lights the cauldron in Atlanta

It's not just the sporting events that can leave us grabbing the tissues. One of the most iconic images in Olympic history is Muhammad Ali lighting the Olympic flame in Atlana in 1996.

The legendary boxer was battling Parkinson's Disease at the time, which meant that lighting the flame was no easy task. The athlete struggled to control his movements however he was able to ignite the torch and set the Olympic cauldron ablaze.



Paula Radcliffe drops out of marathon

British hopeful Paula Radcliffe was the hot favourite as she arrived in Athens in 2004, after she previously made a world record in the marathon distance.

However, the race conditions were extremely warm and Radcliffe never made it to the finish line. She dropped out three miles before the end and broke down in tears on the pavement.

Derek Redmond's injury in Barcelona

British sprinter Derek Redmond headed into the 400m race, but a hamstring injury ended his Olympic dreams in 1992. Determined to finish, Derek started to hobble towards the finish line.

Crying with desperation, his father Jim joined him and helped him finish as each step became more painful. The entire crowd in the stadium rose for a standing ovation and Derek's determination will live forever in the minds of millions.



Cathy Freeman's Sydney win

In 2000, one of the face of the Olympic Games was Australian sprinter Cathy Freeman. With the weight of the country on her shoulders, she carried the pressure and won gold.

Prior to her victory, she was warned that she would be sent home if she celebrated with Australia and Aboriginal flags. When she won, she continued to celebrate with the same flags.

GB rowers Hunter and Purchase in tears

Veteran broadcaster John Inverdale became emotional after Team GB rowers Mark Hunter and Zac Purchase apologised to viewers for not winning their race.

In London 2012, the lightweight double sculls final saw the Netherlands take the win. Hunter said sorry for "letting everyone down," to which John choked up and said: "You've let nobody down."



Tom Daley's emotional 2020 Gold medal

After missing out on the medals completely in Beijing 2008, diver Tom Daley continued his relentless quest for Olympic victory for the next 12 years.

In Tokyo 2020, he won his first gold medal alongside Matty Lee in the 10m Synchro. He was visibly emotional on the podium and struggled to hold back tears. Daley sadly lost his father to cancer in 2011.

Italy and Qatar sharing Olympic glory

The Olympics is also where the unexpected can happen. Gianmarco Tamberi (Italy) and Mutaz Essa Barshim (Qatar) both showed the true meaning of sportsmanship when they shared the gold medal for Men's High Jump.

When the umpire was set on the decision, Tamberi sunk to his knees and broke down in tears of joy.



Yusra Mardini's Olympic journey

Mardini fled the civil war in Syria with her family and later competed under the refugee team in Tokyo 2020. This was her second Olympics as a swimmer after she joined a club in Germany.

"I represent millions around the world. A refugee is a human being like any other," she said. "A refugee means a person who is seeking a refuge, a place where they want to stay safe. And there is nothing wrong with that.

Matthias Steiner's podium tribute

We use your sign-up to provide content in ways you've consented to and to improve our understanding of you. This may include adverts from us and 3rd parties based on our understanding. You can unsubscribe at any time. Read ourPrivacy Policy

Austrian-German weightlifter Mattias Steiner lost his wife in a car accident in 2007, but this didn't prevent him from competing in the 2008 Olympics.

He ended up winning the gold medal and later stood on the medalists' podium with a photo of his late wife. There wasn't a dry eye in the house.

title: Ten emotional moments from Olympic Games history | Celebrity News | Showbiz & TV | Express.co.uk
author: Alexandra Bullard
description: The Olympic Games takes place every four years and are actively used as a mechanism to unite nations - but quite often we have been moved to tears by the action.
